---
title: "EC K-typing G1/G4 Database Build Protocol"
subtitle: "From bloodstream genome screening to AllTheBacteria expansion"
author: "Dr Ebenezer Foster-Nyarko"
date: today
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    code-copy: true
    highlight-style: github
    self-contained: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
    fontsize: 11pt
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

## Overview {.unnumbered}

This protocol describes the complete construction and validation of a
[Kaptive](https://github.com/klebgenomics/Kaptive)-compatible reference
database for typing *Escherichia coli* Group 1 and Group 4 (G1/G4) capsular
polysaccharide (K) loci. G1/G4 loci use the Wzy-dependent biosynthesis
pathway and are flanked by the conserved chromosomal genes *galF* (upstream)
and *gnd* (downstream).

**Repository:** <https://github.com/efosternyarko/EC-K-typing-G1G4>

::: callout-important
## Sequential typing is mandatory

This database must be used **after** screening with the G2/G3 database.
Run G2/G3 first; apply the G1/G4 database only to untypeable assemblies.

A combined all-groups database causes **wzy-interference**: G1/G4 (wzy-based)
loci consistently outscore G2/G3 (kps-based) loci in direct competition,
rendering genuine G2/G3 loci untypeable. Validated in the Malawi dataset:
82/100 correctly G2/G3-typed assemblies became untypeable with the combined
database.
:::

### Version history

| Version | Loci | Notes |
|---------|------|-------|
| v0.1–v0.3.1 | 93 | Initial BSI isolate set; iterative representative swapping and annotation fixes |
| v0.4 | 93 | Conserved genes stripped (galF, gnd, ugd, wza, wzb, wzc) from all records |
| v0.5 | 93 | KL306 and KL307 replaced with NCBI representatives |
| v0.6 | 93 | Conserved genes restored to 91 loci; KL301 stays stripped |
| v0.7 | — | Deprecated: combined all-groups DB (wzy-interference artefact) |
| v0.8 | 667 | + 574 ATB-derived novel loci (KL392–KL968); KL486/562/812 removed |
| v0.9 | 651 | KL303 rep replaced; 16 further loci removed (12 oversized, KL337 unresolvable, KL446/843/968 merged); 651/651 self-typing, 1126/1126 typeability |

---

## Prerequisites

### Software

| Tool | Version | Purpose |
|------|---------|---------|
| Kaptive | ≥ 3.2.1 | K-locus typing |
| FastKaptive | — | High-throughput typing of large genome collections |
| minimap2 | ≥ 2.22 | Sequence alignment |
| BLAST+ | ≥ 2.2.31 | Flanking gene screening, BLASTp annotation |
| MMseqs2 | 17-b804f | Clustering novel loci |
| pyrodigal | ≥ 3.0 | Gene prediction |
| Biopython | ≥ 1.81 | GenBank parsing/writing |
| LexicMap | 0.8.1 | Targeted genome search (AllTheBacteria) |

Install Python dependencies:

```bash
pip install kaptive pyrodigal biopython pandas
```

### Data resources

- **AllTheBacteria (ATB) v202408** — 888 batch archives at
  `/scratch2/js66/atb_archives/` on M3 HPC (Monash).
  Each archive: `atb.assembly.incr_release.202408.batch.NNN.tar.xz`,
  containing FASTA assemblies named `<BioSample_accession>.fa`.
- **ATB LexicMap index** — `s3://allthebacteria-lexicmap/202408/` (public,
  AWS eu-west-2).
- **Klebsiella K-locus primary reference** — bundled with Kaptive at
  `kaptive/data/Klebsiella_k_locus_primary_reference.gbk`. Used for BLASTp
  gene name transfer.

### Compute infrastructure

- **M3 HPC (Monash):** `ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au`
  Scratch: `/home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/`
  SLURM partition: `comp`
- **AWS:** eu-west-2 region, credentials via `aws configure`

### Local paths (Mac)

```python
BLASTP      = "/Users/lshef4/lshef4_to_copy/anaconda3/envs/my_python.env/bin/blastp"
MAKEBLASTDB = "/Users/lshef4/lshef4_to_copy/anaconda3/envs/my_python.env/bin/makeblastdb"
MINIMAP2    = "/Users/lshef4/lshef4_to_copy/anaconda3/envs/kleborate_test/bin/minimap2"
KLEB_REF_GBK = "/Users/lshef4/Library/Python/3.9/lib/python/site-packages/kaptive/data/Klebsiella_k_locus_primary_reference.gbk"
```

::: callout-warning
## BLAST version compatibility
Use the same `makeblastdb` and `blastp` binary from the same installation.
Mixing versions (e.g. `makeblastdb` 2.17 with `blastp` 2.2.31) causes
database format errors. Both binaries at the path above are v2.2.31.
:::

### Repository layout

```
EC-K-typing-G1G4/
├── DB/
│   ├── EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.9.gbk         # current G1/G4 database (651 loci)
│   ├── EC-K-typing_group2and3_v3.0.0.gbk        # Gladstone G2/G3 database
│   ├── KL_G1G4_mapping.tsv                      # all 651 KL → source assembly
│   ├── novel_kl_summary.tsv                     # KL type, accession, cluster size
│   ├── kaptive_validation_results_v0.9norm.tsv  # intermediate BSI-only validation
│   ├── kaptive_validation_summary_v0.9norm.tsv
│   ├── kl300_303_fastas/                        # validated ATB assemblies for KL300/303
│   ├── atb_lexicmap_results/                    # LexicMap output (raw)
│   ├── blast_ncbi_results/                      # NCBI candidate genome FASTAs
│   └── novel_rep_fastas/                        # ATB rep FASTAs for 559 novel loci
├── flanking_genes/
│   └── flanking_genes.fasta              # galF + gnd reference sequences
└── scripts/                              # All pipeline scripts
```

---

## Part A — Building the Initial Database from Bloodstream Isolates (v0.1–v0.6)

### Background

The initial database was built from 6,673 bloodstream infection (BSI)
*E. coli* genomes obtained from EnteroBase. These isolates produced no hit
against the existing Gladstone G2/G3 Kaptive database (kps-based loci), and
were hypothesised to carry G1/G4 (wzy-based) capsular loci. The working
directory for this phase is:

```
/Users/lshef4/Documents/dropbox/work_2025/e_coli_db/db_build_v2/
├── nohits_genomes/     # 1,112 G2/G3 no-hit assemblies (FASTA)
├── extracted_loci/     # locus sequences extracted per assembly
├── extraction_summary.tsv
└── G1_and_G4_fastkaptive_summary.csv
```

---

### Phase 1 — FastKaptive Screening and Candidate Selection

**Objective:** Identify the subset of 6,673 BSI genomes carrying G1/G4 loci.

1. All 6,673 assemblies were typed with FastKaptive against the G2/G3
   reference database.
2. Assemblies returning no confident G2/G3 type were retained as G1/G4
   candidates: **1,112 assemblies** (`nohits_genomes/`).
3. FastKaptive KX type assignments were recorded in
   `G1_and_G4_fastkaptive_summary.csv`, mapping each assembly to a preliminary
   KX locus type.

---

### Phase 2 — Locus Extraction and Initial Clustering (v0.1)

**Script:** `build_G1G4_db.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/build_G1G4_db.py
```

For each of the 1,112 candidate assemblies:

1. **BLASTn flanking genes** (*galF*, *gnd*, *ugd*, *wza*, *wzb*, *wzc*)
   against the assembly (e-value ≤1×10^-10^)
2. **Extract the cps locus region** between *galF* and *gnd*, handling both
   same-contig and cross-contig cases
3. Filter extractions: 5,000–60,000 bp retained

Extracted loci then clustered at **95% identity / 80% coverage** using
CD-HIT-EST (later replaced by MMseqs2 for the ATB phase). The longest
sequence per cluster was selected as the cluster representative.

**Result:** 125 initial loci (KX01–KX125). Of these, **93 were retained**
in the filtered database based on validation performance and biological
evidence; the remaining 32 were excluded as redundant or ambiguous types.

---

### Phase 3 — Annotation and Iterative Validation (v0.1–v0.3.1)

**Script:** `annotate_loci.py`

CDS prediction used pyrodigal (metagenomic mode), with gene names transferred
by BLASTp against the Klebsiella K-locus primary reference.

GenBank records were constructed in Kaptive-compatible format (see
@sec-gbk-format) and validated by self-typing: each representative assembly
must type to its expected KL when the full database is used as the reference.

Across v0.1→v0.3.1, representative assemblies were iteratively swapped for
better alternatives (`swap_reps_v04.py`) and annotation coordinates corrected.
By v0.3.1, **88/93 loci** correctly self-typed.

Remaining failures at v0.3.1: **KL300, KL301, KL303, KL306, KL307** — all
members of the KX01 clade, which shares identical conserved flanking/export
genes (*wza*, *wzb*, *wzc*, *galF*, *gnd*, *ugd*) with KL302. These shared
genes contribute equal bitscore to every KX01 reference, masking the
discriminating variable-region signal.

---

### Phase 4 — Conserved Gene Stripping (v0.4)

**Script:** `make_v04_db.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/make_v04_db.py
```

All conserved CDS features (*wza*, *wzb*, *wzc*, *galF*, *galF_2*, *gnd*,
*ugd*) were removed from all 93 G1/G4 GenBank records. Kaptive then scored
exclusively on discriminating biosynthetic genes.

**Result:** KL301 now correctly self-typed (88 → **89/93**). KL300, KL303,
KL306, and KL307 remained failures.

---

### Phase 5 — KL306/KL307 Replacement with NCBI Representatives (v0.5)

**Script:** `make_v05_db.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/make_v05_db.py
```

`blast_ncbi_candidates.py` was used to identify NCBI chromosome sequences
that type unambiguously as KL306 or KL307:

| Locus | Old rep | New rep | Result |
|-------|---------|---------|--------|
| KL306 | ESC_CC8109AA_AS | CP099041 | 31/31 CDS, Norm AS 2.000 ✓ |
| KL307 | ESC_NB5901AA_AS | CP070103 | 34/34 CDS, Norm AS 2.000 ✓ |

A KL300 replacement (CP135488) was evaluated but **reverted**: the candidate
pulled many assemblies incorrectly to KL300 due to high similarity with other
KX01 variable regions, causing net regressions in typeability.

KL303 was left unchanged — no NCBI candidate typed unambiguously as KL303;
the variable region was indistinguishable from KL302/KL352 across all tested
assemblies.

**Result:** 89 → **91/93** correctly self-typed.

---

### Phase 6 — Conserved Gene Restoration (v0.6)

**Script:** `make_v06_db.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/make_v06_db.py
```

Conserved genes were restored to 91 of the 93 loci by copying CDS features
from v0.3.1 (coordinates are identical for 91 loci; BLASTn liftover used for
the two loci with new sequences: KL306 and KL307).

| Locus | Action |
|-------|--------|
| KL301 | Conserved genes **kept stripped** — their removal is what fixed KL301 |
| KL306, KL307 | Conserved genes restored via BLASTn liftover onto new sequences |
| All others (91) | Conserved genes restored directly from v0.3.1 |

**Final v0.6 result:** **91/93 correctly self-typed.** Remaining failures:
KL300 and KL303 only.

Output: `DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.6.gbk` (93 loci, G1/G4 only)

---

## Part B — AllTheBacteria Expansion (v0.6 → v0.8)

### Phase 7 — ATB Screening for G1/G4 Candidates

**Objective:** Identify ATB assemblies containing G1/G4 *cps* loci by
detecting the conserved flanking genes *galF* and *gnd*.

**HPC working directory:**
```
/home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/atb_screen/
├── results/          # batch_N_hits.tsv files
├── novel_loci/       # extracted novel locus FASTAs
├── logs/
├── atb_g1g4_candidates.tsv
└── batches_with_candidates.txt
```

#### Step 7.1 — Screen all 888 ATB batches (SLURM array)

**Scripts:** `submit_atb_screen.sh` → `atb_screen_batch.py`

```bash
# On M3:
cd /home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/atb_screen
sbatch /home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/submit_atb_screen.sh
```

Each array task (`1–888`, max 50 concurrent):

1. Extracts all assemblies from the `.tar.xz` archive into a temp directory
2. Builds a combined FASTA
3. BLASTn against `flanking_genes/flanking_genes.fasta` (*galF* + *gnd*)
4. Writes `results/batch_N_hits.tsv`

**BLASTn thresholds:** ≥80% nucleotide identity, ≥70% query coverage,
e-value ≤1×10^-10^.

```bash
# Monitor:
squeue -u ebenezef
ls results/batch_*_hits.tsv | wc -l   # expect 665 with candidates
```

#### Step 7.2 — Combine screening results

**Script:** `combine_atb_hits.py`

```bash
python3 /home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/combine_atb_hits.py
```

Filters for genomes with **both** *galF* AND *gnd* hits on the **same contig**.
Outputs:

- `atb_g1g4_candidates.tsv` — genomes to extract loci from
- `batches_with_candidates.txt` — batch numbers containing ≥1 candidate

---

### Phase 8 — Novel Locus Extraction

**Objective:** Extract locus sequences between *galF* and *gnd*, discarding
sequences that match the existing reference database.

#### Step 8.1 — Build novelty-screening BLAST database

**Script:** `setup_novelty_db.sh` *(run once)*

```bash
bash /home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/setup_novelty_db.sh
```

Builds a BLAST nucleotide database from `DB/reference_loci_v0.6.fasta`
(93 existing loci) at:
`/home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/atb_screen/novelty_db/g1g4_refs`

#### Step 8.2 — Extract novel loci (SLURM array)

**Scripts:** `submit_extract_novel.sh` → `extract_novel_loci.py`

```bash
sbatch /home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/submit_extract_novel.sh
```

Each array task (`1–268`, max 50 concurrent):

1. Loads same-contig candidates for the batch
2. BLASTn each candidate contig against the novelty DB:
   - ≥95% identity AND ≥80% coverage → **known type, skip**
   - Otherwise → **novel candidate**
3. BLASTn *galF* and *gnd* against the contig to get precise coordinates
4. Extracts the region between *galF* end and *gnd* start, ±500 bp flanking
5. Discards extractions <15,000 bp (likely partial/fragmented)

**Result:** 347,524 novel locus sequences across 268 batches.

---

### Phase 9 — Clustering Novel Loci

**Objective:** Collapse 347,524 raw sequences into unique KL types.

#### Step 9.1 — Run MMseqs2 clustering

**Script:** `cluster_novel_loci.sh`

```bash
sbatch /home/ebenezef/js66_scratch/ebenn/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/cluster_novel_loci.sh
```

MMseqs2 parameters:

```bash
mmseqs cluster \
    "$MMSEQS_DB" "$MMSEQS_CLUDB" "$MMSEQS_TMP" \
    --min-seq-id 0.95 \    # 95% nucleotide identity
    -c 0.80 \              # 80% coverage
    --cov-mode 0 \         # bidirectional coverage
    --cluster-mode 0 \     # greedy set cover
    --threads 16
```

#### Step 9.2 — Assign KL type numbers

Cluster representatives sorted by descending cluster size (most prevalent
types receive the lowest KL numbers):

```python
# assign_novel_kl_types.py (run on M3)
KL_START = 392   # v0.6 highest existing locus is KL391
```

::: callout-warning
## KL numbering correction

The `cluster_novel_loci.sh` script header references KL344 as the start.
This is incorrect — the v0.6 database already contains loci up to **KL391**.
New types must start at **KL392**. The `assign_novel_kl_types.py` script
used `KL_START = 392`.
:::

**Result:** 577 novel KL types assigned (KL392–KL968).

Output files (M3):
```
atb_screen/clustering/
├── novel_kl_types.fasta       # representative sequences
└── novel_kl_summary.tsv       # KL type, accession, size, cluster count
```

---

### Phase 10 — Annotation and Database Build (v0.8)

**Objective:** Predict and annotate CDS in novel loci; merge with v0.6 → v0.8.

**Script:** `annotate_and_merge_atb_loci.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/annotate_and_merge_atb_loci.py 2>&1 | tee /tmp/annotate_v08.log
```

Pipeline steps:

1. **Partial-capture screening:** BLASTn each novel locus against v0.6.
   Reject if ≥80% of the novel sequence aligns at ≥95% identity to any
   existing locus.
2. **CDS prediction:** pyrodigal in metagenomic mode (`meta=True`)
3. **BLASTp annotation:** predicted proteins vs Klebsiella K-locus primary
   reference; transfer gene names at ≥30% identity, ≥50% query coverage
4. **GenBank record construction** (Kaptive-compatible format; see @sec-gbk-format)
5. **Merge:** annotated novel records appended to v0.6 GenBank → v0.8

**Result:** 670 records (93 + 577), 57.2% CDS annotated, all passed
Kaptive format validation.

---

### Phase 11 — Self-typing Validation (Original 93 Loci)

**Objective:** Confirm each of the original 93 BSI loci correctly types
to its expected KL against the complete v0.8 database.

#### Step 11.1 — Run normalised scoring validation

**Script:** `type_normalized.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/type_normalized.py \
    --db DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.8.gbk \
    --suffix v0.8norm
```

::: callout-note
## Deleting stale scores files

Kaptive `--scores` **appends** to an existing TSV. Always delete the
scores file before re-running:

```bash
rm -f DB/kaptive_scores_v0.8norm.tsv
```
:::

**Initial result:** 88/93 correctly typed.
Failures: KL300, KL303, KL312, KL388, KL391.

#### Step 11.2 — Diagnose and fix regressions

BLASTn analysis revealed three novel loci were partial captures of
existing reference loci:

| Novel locus removed | Identity | Coverage | Duplicate of |
|---------------------|----------|----------|--------------|
| KL486 | 100% | 97% | KL312 |
| KL562 | 99.8% | 100% | KL391 |
| KL812 | 99.2% | 100% | KL388 |

These were removed from the v0.8 GenBank (667 records). The partial-capture
screening step in `annotate_and_merge_atb_loci.py` was updated to catch these
cases in future runs.

**Final v0.8 result:** **91/93 correctly typed, 100% typeability** (567/567
assemblies typeable). Remaining failures: KL300 and KL303 only.

---

### Phase 12 — Novel Loci Self-typing Validation on M3

**Objective:** Validate that each of the 574 novel loci (KL392–KL968) can
be correctly typed from its ATB representative assembly against the complete
v0.8 database.

#### Step 12.1 — Upload files to M3

```bash
scp DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.8.gbk \
    ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/DB/

scp atb_clustering/novel_kl_summary.tsv \
    ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/DB/

scp scripts/validate_novel_loci_m3.sh \
    scripts/normalise_novel_scores.py \
    ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/
```

#### Step 12.2 — Submit validation job

**Script:** `validate_novel_loci_m3.sh`

```bash
sbatch ~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/validate_novel_loci_m3.sh
```

The script:

1. Installs Kaptive v3 (`pip install --user "kaptive>=3.0"`)
2. Loads `module load minimap2/2.28`
3. Scans candidate archives (`tar tf`) to locate representative accessions
4. Extracts FASTAs grouped by archive for efficiency into `DB/novel_rep_fastas/`
5. Runs `kaptive assembly --scores` against v0.8
6. Runs `normalise_novel_scores.py` to assess self-typing correctness

```bash
# Monitor:
tail -f ~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/logs/val_novel_JOBID.log
```

#### Step 12.3 — Retrieve results and rep FASTAs

To enable full 667-loci self-validation locally, download the novel
representative FASTAs from M3 after the job completes:

```bash
mkdir -p DB/novel_rep_fastas

scp -r ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:\
~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/DB/novel_rep_fastas/ \
    DB/

scp ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:\
~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/DB/novel_validation_summary_v0.8norm.tsv \
    DB/
```

---

## Part C — Resolving KL300 and KL303 (v0.8 → v0.9)

**Background:** KL300 and KL303 share the KX01 polysaccharide gene cluster
with KL302. The original BSI representative assemblies for these two loci
type to KL302 (or related loci) under normalised scoring. A targeted LexicMap
search across all of ATB was required to find assemblies that unambiguously
carry KL300 or KL303.

---

### Phase 13 — LexicMap Search on EC2

**Script:** `run_atb_lexicmap_ec2.sh`

```bash
bash scripts/run_atb_lexicmap_ec2.sh
```

Launches an `r7g.4xlarge` on-demand EC2 instance (16 vCPU, 128 GB RAM,
ARM64, eu-west-2) and runs LexicMap against the ATB S3 index:

```bash
lexicmap search \
    -d /mnt/atb_index \
    /home/ec2-user/query.fasta \
    -o /home/ec2-user/results.tsv.gz \
    --align-min-match-pident 90 \
    --min-qcov-per-genome 70 \
    --top-n-genomes 0 \
    -j 16
```

Query: `DB/kl_failing_query.fasta` (KL300: 45,380 bp; KL303: 40,992 bp).
Runtime: ~5h52min. Cost: ~$0.80 total.

**Check progress:**

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/atb_search_key ec2-user@<PUBLIC_IP> \
    'tail -20 /home/ec2-user/lexicmap_run.log'

ssh -i ~/.ssh/atb_search_key ec2-user@<PUBLIC_IP> \
    'test -f /home/ec2-user/DONE && echo DONE || echo "Still running"'
```

**Fetch results:**

```bash
bash scripts/fetch_atb_results.sh <INSTANCE_ID> <PUBLIC_IP>
```

::: callout-note
## EC2 troubleshooting notes

- Spot quota = 0 on new AWS accounts → use on-demand
- Default vCPU limit = 16 → use `r7g.4xlarge` not `c7g.8xlarge`
- User-data 25,600 byte limit → do not embed large FASTA; SCP after instance boots
- FUSE mount (`/mnt/atb_index`) owned by root → run LexicMap via `sudo nohup bash`
- `fetch_atb_results.sh`: pass INSTANCE_ID and PUBLIC_IP as arguments directly
:::

**Analyse results:**

```bash
python3 scripts/analyse_atb_results.py
```

**Results:** 16,324,589 alignment rows processed.

| Locus | Candidates | Top candidates (99.8% qcov, 100% pident) |
|-------|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| KL300 | 329,240 | SAMD00053151, SAMEA103908486, SAMN12532904 |
| KL303 | 221,599 | SAMEA6656333, SAMEA6656328, SAMN20959615 |

---

### Phase 14 — Validate KL300/KL303 Candidates on M3

**Script:** `validate_kl300_kl303_m3.sh`

```bash
scp scripts/validate_kl300_kl303_m3.sh \
    ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/

# On M3:
sbatch ~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/scripts/validate_kl300_kl303_m3.sh
```

The script downloads assemblies for the 6 top candidates directly from ENA
portal API, NCBI datasets, and ATB S3 (in order of priority) — extracting
from M3 archives is not possible as only 223 of 888 ATB batches are downloaded.

```bash
# Monitor:
tail -f ~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/logs/val_kl300_303_JOBID.log
```

**Validation results (job 52816827):**

| Accession | Expected | Result | Status |
|-----------|----------|--------|--------|
| SAMD00053151 | KL300 | KL957 | ✗ |
| SAMEA103908486 | KL300 | KL668 | ✗ |
| SAMN12532904 | KL300 | KL957 | ✗ |
| SAMEA6656333 | KL303 | KL303 | ✓ |
| SAMEA6656328 | KL303 | KL303 | ✓ |
| SAMN20959615 | KL303 | KL570 | ✗ |

::: callout-important
## KL300 unresolved

All three KL300 candidates typed to different novel loci (KL957 ×2, KL668 ×1).
The LexicMap hits were driven by sequence shared between the v0.8 KL300
reference and these novel loci (likely conserved biosynthetic genes), not
by a specific KL300 match. The v0.8 KL300 GenBank record remains in place
but its sequence origin requires further investigation. A wzy-targeted
LexicMap search is recommended as a next step.
:::

---

### Phase 15 — Build v0.9

#### Step 15.1 — Fetch validated KL303 FASTA from M3

```bash
mkdir -p DB/kl300_303_fastas

scp ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:\
~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/DB/kl300_303_fastas/SAMEA6656333.fa \
    DB/kl300_303_fastas/
```

Only SAMEA6656333 is fetched; no valid KL300 assembly was identified.

#### Step 15.2 — Extract locus and replace KL303 in GenBank

**Script:** `replace_kl300_kl303.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/replace_kl300_kl303.py \
    --kl303_fasta DB/kl300_303_fastas/SAMEA6656333.fa
```

The script accepts either or both loci (`--kl300_fasta`, `--kl303_fasta`,
or `--auto`). With only KL303 specified, only KL303 is replaced.

Steps performed:

1. Loads existing KL303 sequence from v0.8 as minimap2 query (`asm5` preset)
2. Aligns to SAMEA6656333; extracts best-covered region ±500 bp
3. Re-annotates with pyrodigal + BLASTp vs Klebsiella reference
4. Replaces KL303 record; writes `DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.9.gbk`

**Result:** KL303 replaced with 34,424 bp from `SAMEA6656333.contig00015`
(38 CDS predicted, 5 annotated against Klebsiella reference).

::: callout-note
## Script fixes applied (Python 3.9 compatibility)

- `str | None` union syntax replaced with `Optional[str]` (requires 3.10+)
- `MINIMAP2` path hardcoded to conda env binary
- `MAKEBLASTDB` path hardcoded to match `BLASTP` version (both v2.2.31)
:::

#### Step 15.3 — Update canonical representative mapping

The original mapping files (`KL_G1G4_mapping.tsv`, `KL_G1G4_mapping_filtered.tsv`)
listed representatives that type to incorrect loci:

| Locus | Old rep | Typed as | New rep | Typed as |
|-------|---------|----------|---------|----------|
| KL300 | ESC_GC2276AA_AS | KL302 | ESC_CC1364AA_AS | KL300 ✓ |
| KL303 | ESC_JB0772AA_AS | KL570 | ESC_UB1439AA_AS | KL303 ✓ |

Both mapping files updated manually. The old representatives are genuine
members of other locus types, not mistyped KL300/KL303 assemblies.

#### Step 15.4 — Intermediate validation (BSI loci only)

```bash
python3 scripts/type_normalized.py \
    --db DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.9.gbk \
    --suffix v0.9norm
```

This step validated the original 92 BSI loci to confirm the KL303
replacement had no regressions. Novel ATB loci are not yet included
(representative FASTAs not yet available locally at this point).

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Filtered BSI loci self-typing | **92/92 (100%)** |
| Typeability (567 BSI assemblies) | 567/567 (100%) |

::: callout-note
## Novel loci partially visible at this stage

The `KL_G1G4_mapping.tsv` at this stage included the original 93 BSI
loci plus any ATB reps that had been downloaded for KL300/KL303
investigation. The 559 novel ATB loci were not yet in the mapping file
and were not validated here.
:::

---

### Phase 15.5 — Remove Problematic Loci (v0.8 → v0.9 cleanup)

**Objective:** Remove novel loci that caused cross-typing interference
or were indistinguishable from other types, reducing the database from
667 to 651 records.

#### Step 15.5.1 — Remove oversized loci (>60 CDS)

**Script:** `remove_oversized_loci.py`

```bash
python3 scripts/remove_oversized_loci.py --cds-threshold 60
```

Novel ATB extractions with >60 predicted CDS captured extensive flanking
chromosomal sequence. These loci universally outscored legitimate loci
because their conserved chromosomal genes are present in every assembly.
The original 93 BSI loci have a maximum of 53 CDS (KL308), so 60 is a
clean cut-off.

**12 loci removed:** KL649, KL694, KL731, KL828, KL837, KL840, KL851,
KL869, KL879, KL880, KL930, KL957.

#### Step 15.5.2 — Remove unresolvable and merged loci (manual)

The following four further loci were removed from the GenBank by direct
deletion of their records:

| Locus | Reason |
|-------|--------|
| KL337 | No assembly in the validation set types uniquely to KL337; `ESC_IA2252AA_AS` (the representative) scores marginally higher for KL389 (both are KX36 loci with near-identical gene sets) |
| KL446 | Merged into KL443 — both cluster representatives produced identical AS scores across the full validation set; KL446 gene set is a subset of KL443 |
| KL843 | Merged into KL830 — same reason as KL446/KL443 |
| KL968 | Merged into KL716 — same reason as KL446/KL443 |

::: callout-note
## Subset-locus merging rationale

Where two loci have identical gene sets (one being a strict subset of
the other), Kaptive cannot distinguish them. Retaining both causes
unpredictable assignment depending on assembly completeness. The correct
action is to keep the locus with the larger, more complete reference and
remove the subset locus from the database.
:::

**Total removed in Phase 15.5:** 16 loci (667 → 651).

---

### Phase 16 — Full 651-Loci Self-typing Validation

**Objective:** Validate all 651 loci (92 BSI + 559 novel ATB) using
representative assemblies for the novel loci, which were downloaded from
M3 after the `validate_novel_loci_m3.sh` job completed.

#### Step 16.1 — Download novel rep FASTAs from M3

```bash
mkdir -p DB/novel_rep_fastas

scp -r ebenezef@m3.massive.org.au:\
~/EC-K-typing-G1G4/DB/novel_rep_fastas/ \
    DB/
```

#### Step 16.2 — Run full validation

**Script:** `type_normalized.py`

```bash
rm -f DB/kaptive_scores_v0.9norm_full.tsv

python3 scripts/type_normalized.py \
    --db DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.9.gbk \
    --suffix v0.9norm_full
```

The script loads representative assemblies from three sources:

- `db_build_v2/nohits_genomes/` — original 92 BSI representative assemblies
- `DB/blast_ncbi_results/candidate_genomes/` — NCBI-sourced reps (KL306, KL307, KL303)
- `DB/novel_rep_fastas/` — ATB representative FASTAs for 559 novel loci

The `KL_G1G4_mapping.tsv` and `novel_rep_kl_map.tsv` were extended to
include the novel ATB loci, enabling the script to locate the correct
FASTA for each.

#### Step 16.3 — Results

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| **All 651 loci self-typing** | **651/651 (100%)** |
| BSI loci (92) | 92/92 (100%) |
| Novel ATB loci (559) | 559/559 (100%) |
| **Typeability (1,126 assemblies)** | **1,126/1,126 (100%)** |

Validation against the [Gladstone et al. 2024](https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.11.22.24317484v1)
Malawi dataset confirmed these results on an independent collection.

Output files:

```
DB/kaptive_validation_results_v0.9norm_full.tsv
DB/kaptive_validation_summary_v0.9norm_full.tsv
```

---

## Technical Appendices

### kpsM Pre-screen (Alternative to Sequential G2/G3 Kaptive) {#sec-kpsm}

**Scripts:** `kpsm_screen.py`, `validate_kpsm_screen.py`

The standard workflow runs the full Gladstone G2/G3 Kaptive database on all
assemblies first and passes untypeables to the G1/G4 database. For large
collections, a faster pre-screen uses `kpsM` as a Group 2/3 marker gene:

- `kpsM` encodes an inner membrane ABC-transporter component present in all
  Group 2 strains; `kpsM_3` is the Group 3 paralogue. Both are absent from
  Group 1/4 strains.
- Detection of either gene (≥90% identity, ≥80% coverage by BLASTn) identifies
  a Group 2/3 assembly; G1/G4 Kaptive typing is suppressed for these.

#### Usage

```bash
# Screen a directory of assemblies and pass kpsM-negative ones to Kaptive:
python3 scripts/kpsm_screen.py \
    --assembly-dir assemblies/ \
    --kpsm-ref DB/kpsM_reference.fasta \
    --kaptive-db DB/EC-K-typing_group1and4_v0.9.gbk \
    --output-dir results/kpsm_screen/ \
    --run-kaptive

# Screen only (no Kaptive):
python3 scripts/kpsm_screen.py \
    --assembly-dir assemblies/ \
    --kpsm-ref DB/kpsM_reference.fasta \
    --output-dir results/kpsm_screen/
```

**Output:** `kpsm_screen_results/kpsm_screen_results.tsv` — one row per
assembly with columns `assembly`, `kpsM_hit`, `pident`, `qcov`, `group`
(`Group2_3` or `G1_G4_candidate`).

#### Validation

`validate_kpsm_screen.py` runs a batch kpsM screen over a directory of
assemblies using `blastn -subject` (no `makeblastdb` step per genome),
parallelised with `multiprocessing`. Used during development to confirm
that all 1,112 BSI no-hit assemblies were kpsM-negative:

```bash
python3 scripts/validate_kpsm_screen.py \
    --genome-dir db_build_v2/nohits_genomes/ \
    --kpsm-ref DB/kpsM_reference.fasta \
    --output results/kpsm_validation.tsv \
    --workers 8
```

::: callout-note
## kpsM screen vs sequential Kaptive

The kpsM screen is faster than running the full G2/G3 Kaptive database
(BLASTn vs minimap2 whole-locus alignment) but provides less information —
it identifies Group 2/3 organisms without assigning a K-locus type.
Use kpsM pre-screening when you need to segregate G1/G4 candidates quickly
before running the G1/G4 Kaptive database. Use sequential Kaptive when you
also need G2/G3 K-locus assignments.
:::

---

### Kaptive GenBank Format Requirements {#sec-gbk-format}

Each record must follow Kaptive's expected format exactly:

```
LOCUS       KL392_ATB    45123 bp    DNA
...
FEATURES
  source    1..45123
            /note="K locus: KL392"
            /organism="Escherichia coli"
            /mol_type="genomic DNA"
  CDS       complement(100..1200)
            /gene="wzy"
            /product="Wzy polymerase"
            /translation="MAST..."
```

The `source` feature **must** contain `/note="K locus: KLxxx"` — this is
how Kaptive identifies the locus name at runtime.

### Normalised Scoring

**Script:** `type_normalized.py`

Raw Kaptive `--scores` output provides, per assembly per locus: `AS`
(alignment score), `genes_found`, `genes_expected`, `q_len`.

**Rationale:** Raw AS is proportional to locus size. A large locus always
outscores a small one even at lower fractional coverage. Normalising by
total expected CDS length gives a size-independent comparison:

$$\text{AS\_norm} = \frac{\text{AS}}{\sum \text{CDS length for locus}}$$

**Assignment algorithm (v0.9):**

1. Compute AS_norm for all loci with AS > 0
2. Sort loci by AS_norm descending; use raw AS as tiebreaker (descending)
3. Assign the top-ranked locus as the best match
4. Mark **Typeable** if `genes_found / genes_expected ≥ 0.50`

::: callout-note
## Algorithm change from v0.8 to v0.9

Earlier versions used a 0.5 % shortlist window (AS_norm ≥ top × 0.995)
and resolved ties by `genes_found`. This caused pathological behaviour
with subset loci — a locus that finds all its (fewer) genes at 100 %
identity could outscore a larger correct locus after removing subset
types improved coverage. The direct AS_norm + raw AS sort eliminates
this: when both AS_norm values are equal (e.g. both 2.000), the locus
with more total aligned evidence (higher raw AS, i.e. more genes) wins.
:::

### Key Design Decisions

**Why G1/G4 only, not combined?**
A combined all-groups database was tested (v0.7) but deprecated due to
wzy-interference. Confirmed in three independent datasets (Malawi, Norway,
UK). Sequential typing (G2/G3 first) is mandatory.

**KL numbering start: KL392, not KL344.**
The `cluster_novel_loci.sh` script header incorrectly states KL344 as the
start. The v0.6 database contains loci up to KL391; new loci must start
at KL392.

**Bidirectional clustering (--cov-mode 0).**
Ensures both query and target meet the 80% coverage threshold. Prevents
short sequences from clustering into larger, unrelated loci. Partial
captures shorter than the reference can still pass — handled by the
additional BLASTn screening step in `annotate_and_merge_atb_loci.py`.

**Minimum locus length: 15,000 bp.**
Novel extractions below this threshold are discarded as likely partial due
to contig breaks within the locus.

**KL300 status.**
KL300 remains unresolved. The LexicMap candidates typed to novel loci
(KL957, KL668), indicating the v0.8 KL300 reference shares substantial
sequence with these loci. Recommended next step: extract only the *wzy*
gene from the KL300 record and use it as the LexicMap query to find
assemblies with a KL300-specific *wzy*, avoiding hits driven by conserved
flanking or biosynthetic genes shared with other loci.

**KL303 replacement.**
ATB assembly SAMEA6656333 was validated as a true KL303 representative
(Kaptive normalised score 2.000, 38/38 CDS). The locus was extracted from
`SAMEA6656333.contig00015` (34,424 bp) using minimap2 with the v0.8 KL303
sequence as query.
